Xbox Save Games

A save game is the generic term used for data saved by players, data that players associate with game progress or results. A save game could represent many different types of data, including:

  • Keys for unlocking new or secret game features: A simple method of saving game progress used by many console games today.
  • Game progress: The standard save game concept. Saving the current game state so that the player can resume from that point at a later time.
  • Game customizations: Saving player-customized game data, such as assembling a hand-tuned football team, updating a vehicle's equipment or capabilities, and so on.
  • Game replays: Saving a replay of the game action for later viewing.
  • Screen shots: Static screen shots showing the action at a specific moment.
  • Many more possibilities.

Save games are organized as container directories in a title's user data region. Each save game is a unique named directory in the root of a user data region on a given device (hard disk or memory unit). Within the save game container directory are the data and metadata files representing a specific save game.

The data files within the save game directory are specific to the title and opaque to the Xbox. There can also be metadata files that contain game data, such as textures and graphics, used by the Xbox Dashboard to brand the game for the user.

At any given time, a collection of storage devices that may contain save games is connected to the Xbox and available to the title. This will always include the hard disk drive and may additionally include a number of Xbox memory units. Save games are stored and retrieved from these devices as needed by the title. Note that there is no guarantee that a game saved on a memory unit has ever been played on the console to which the memory unit is currently connected. The memory units will commonly be used to transfer save games from one console to another.

Creating and Managing Save Games

The basic unit of save game manipulation is a subdirectory within the title's user data region on a given device. The Xbox Dashboard moves and deletes save game data only at this level. This hierarchy provides enough structure to manage save game data out of title, while still giving titles maximum freedom in determining the exact format and content of save games. Within each save game subdirectory, a title can write desired game data in whatever format it wishes.

It is recommended that titles use no more than 8 MB of data to represent one save game. This ensures save game roamability between the hard disk and the memory units. Some certification requirements relate to using save game container directories and limiting save games to 8 MB.

Save Game Functions

Several XTL functions are provided to simplify the tasks involved in managing save games. These functions aid in creating, deleting, and enumerating save games. They are low-overhead functions that perform the minimum tasks necessary to manage save games using the defined hierarchy.

Creating and Deleting Save Games describes the process of creating and deleting save games.

Enumerating Save Games discusses the functions used to enumerate existing save games.

Save Game Metadata

Various types of metadata will be defined for the Xbox. Titles can then optionally associate this metadata with a saved game. Metadata lives in the save game directory, and each metadata file will have the standard extension *.xbx. To ensure that future .xbx file names do not collide with those of the title, the title should not create proprietary .xbx files. The title can store, as metadata, images associated with save games and with the title itself. The Xbox Dashboard can use this metadata to display this graphical information to the user.

In addition to creating metadata within a particular save game, titles can save common metadata that applies to all save games on a particular device. All .xbx files saved to a particular title's root directory in the save game region of a device are considered common metadata files. Thus, a title that associates a particular model with all save games could save space and time by saving the metadata to the title's root directory. It is the responsibility of the title to ensure that common metadata exists (if needed) when saving a game to a device. If a user copies a game from one device to another from within the Xbox Dashboard, appropriate common metadata will also be transferred if it does not already exist on the target device.

Details of metadata content and format can be found in the Dashboard Metadata topic.
